figures.gloss: The Supreme Personality of Godhead in A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada's ISKCON. He is not an avatar of Vishnu, but the absolute, original source of all avatars, universes, and spiritual energies, reigning in the highest spiritual planet, Goloka Vrindavana.
figures.facet: ISKCON radically inverts Advaita Vedanta: the formless, infinite Brahman is merely the blinding rays of Krishna's localized, blue-hued, flute-playing transcendental body. Personality is superior to formlessness.
figures.provenance: A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada's 'Bhagavad-gita As It Is' (1968).

figures.gloss: The Supreme Soul in the Brahma Kumaris tradition. He is an incorporeal point of divine, reddish-golden light dwelling in the Soul World (Paramdham), entirely beyond the cycle of birth, death, and human degradation.
figures.facet: He is completely stripped of traditional Hindu mythological iconography. He never incarnates biologically, but merely possesses the body of the founder, Brahma Baba, to speak directly to the 'Brahmin' initiates.
figures.provenance: Lekhraj Kripalani's channeled 'Murlis' (1930s-present).

figures.gloss: Maharishi Mahesh Yogi's reinterpretation of pure consciousness. It is the fundamental, unmanifest level of nature's intelligence from which all matter and energy arise, accessible directly through the effortless mental technique of Transcendental Meditation.
figures.facet: Spiritual liberation is explicitly scientized and stripped of religious devotion. Achieving cosmic consciousness is framed as a measurable neurophysiological shift governed by quantum physics and natural law.
figures.provenance: Maharishi Mahesh Yogi's 'Science of Being and Art of Living' (1963).

figures.gloss: Osho's vision of the ultimate 'New Man'—an integrated being who completely unites the sensual, earthly joyousness of 'Zorba the Greek' with the silent, detached awareness of Gautama Buddha.
figures.facet: It violently rejects the foundational asceticism of traditional Indian monasticism. Enlightenment is achieved not by renouncing wealth, sex, and material pleasure, but by moving through them with total, meditative awareness.
figures.provenance: Bhagwan Shree Rajneesh's (Osho) 'Zorba the Buddha' discourses (1970s-80s).

figures.gloss: The primordial, maternal spiritual energy lying dormant at the base of the spine. In Shri Mataji Nirmala Devi's Sahaja Yoga, it is awakened to pierce the fontanel, connecting the individual to the all-pervading power of divine love.
figures.facet: Historically a dangerous, esoteric fire requiring decades of extreme ascetic preparation, it is democratized into instant, mass 'Self-Realization' bestowed freely in public auditoriums with no prior purification.
figures.provenance: Shri Mataji Nirmala Devi's 'Meta Modern Era' (1995).

figures.gloss: The deathless, supreme Himalayan master of the Self-Realization Fellowship lineage. Retaining the physical form of a twenty-five-year-old youth for centuries, he operates in secret to guide the spiritual evolution of humanity.
figures.facet: He acts as a historical bridge, deliberately blending ancient Indian yogic science with Western esoteric expectations of ascended masters, transmitting the specific salvation mechanism of 'Kriya Yoga' to the modern world.
figures.provenance: Paramahansa Yogananda's 'Autobiography of a Yogi' (1946).

figures.gloss: In Ananda Marga, the rare, singular incarnation of the Supreme Entity who physically enters the material world when dharma is entirely lost, acting as both a spiritual preceptor and a fierce social liberator.
figures.facet: Unlike a passive guru, this figure demands radical, militant socio-economic revolution (PROUT) alongside deep tantric meditation, destroying oppressive capitalist and communist structures to establish a moral, spiritual society.
figures.provenance: Prabhat Ranjan Sarkar's 'Discourses on Tantra' (1950s-90s).

figures.gloss: The Brahma Kumaris' conceptualization of cosmic time as an eternally repeating, identical World Drama. Humanity begins in a pure Golden Age, degrading through Silver, Copper, and Iron ages before the apocalyptic destruction and immediate renewal.
figures.facet: The traditional Hindu cycle of millions of years is hyper-compressed into exactly 5,000 years. It is absolute determinism: every single soul repeats their exact thoughts and actions every cycle, forever.
figures.provenance: Brahma Kumaris' core curriculum and channeled 'Murlis' (1930s-present).

figures.gloss: Ramakrishna Paramahamsa (1836-1886), the Dakshineswar Kali temple priest whose visionary trance-states (bhava samadhi) and successive practice of Islamic and Christian devotional disciplines led him to declare that all paths reach the same divine goal. Venerated by the Ramakrishna Math and Mission as an avatara for the present age.
figures.facet: His authority rests entirely on reported ecstatic experience rather than scripture or lineage — the movement built around him had to be organized after his death by disciples precisely because he himself produced no systematic teaching, only trance and aphorism.
figures.provenance: Mahendranath Gupta ('M'), The Gospel of Sri Ramakrishna (recorded 1882-86, published 1902); Ramakrishna Math and Mission institutional literature.

figures.gloss: Narendranath Datta (1863-1902), Ramakrishna's chief monastic disciple, who systematized his master's ecstatic realizations into a portable practical Vedanta and delivered the address to the 1893 World's Parliament of Religions in Chicago that made Hindu teaching a durable presence in the West. Founder of the Ramakrishna Mission (1897) and the Vedanta Society.
figures.facet: He is the template every later guru-to-the-West figure repeats: a single foreign lecture tour converted into a permanent institutional base, so that biography and organization-building become inseparable in this category from Vivekananda onward.
figures.provenance: Vivekananda's Chicago address, 11 September 1893 (Parliament of Religions proceedings); The Complete Works of Swami Vivekananda (compiled posthumously).

figures.gloss: Mukunda Lal Ghosh (1893-1952), founder of the Self-Realization Fellowship (Los Angeles, 1920), who transmitted Kriya Yoga — a guided-breath meditation technique received through his own guru lineage — to a mass American audience. His body's reported freedom from visible decomposition twenty days after death became a devotional cornerstone of SRF teaching.
figures.facet: He packaged the guru-disciple lineage itself as the product being transmitted — Kriya Yoga is presented less as a doctrine to be believed than a technique to be certified and passed hand to hand, a model many later teachers copied.
figures.provenance: Paramahansa Yogananda, Autobiography of a Yogi (1946); Self-Realization Fellowship institutional literature, including the Los Angeles County coroner's statement on his remains (1952).

figures.gloss: Abhay Charan De (1896-1977), a Gaudiya Vaishnava sannyasi who arrived in New York in 1965 and founded the International Society for Krishna Consciousness (ISKCON) the following year. His translations and commentaries ('purports') on the Bhagavad-gita and Bhagavata Purana carry near-scriptural authority in the movement, and his murti receives daily ritual worship (guru-puja) in ISKCON temples worldwide.
figures.facet: Devotion to him did not end at his death but was built to continue past it by design — the daily guru-puja means he remains a present ritual object rather than a remembered founder, structurally closer to a living deity than to a historical biography.
figures.provenance: A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada, Bhagavad-gita As It Is (1968); ISKCON institutional liturgy and temple practice.

figures.gloss: Sathyanarayana Raju (1926-2011), who at age 14 declared himself the reincarnation of the Maharashtrian saint Shirdi Sai Baba and was subsequently venerated by followers as an avatar of Shiva and Shakti combined. Renowned for public materializations of vibhuti (sacred ash) and other objects, his ashram at Puttaparthi drew millions of pilgrims and a global devotee network before his death.
figures.facet: His avatar claim explicitly annexes a prior saint's following into his own — continuity of devotion is manufactured by declared reincarnation rather than by lineage or institutional succession, a distinct mechanism from the guru-to-guru transmission seen elsewhere in this roster.
figures.provenance: Sathya Sai Baba's own discourses and declarations (from 1940); Sathya Sai Central Trust institutional literature; independent journalistic and scholarly accounts of the materialization phenomena and controversies surrounding them.

figures.gloss: Sahajanand Swami (1781-1830), worshipped within the Swaminarayan sampradaya — and prominently within its BAPS branch (founded 1907) — as Purushottam, the supreme divine reality manifest in a human body, ranked above Vishnu's own avatars rather than as one of them.
figures.facet: Placing the founder above Vishnu's avatar-hierarchy, rather than folding him into it as one more avatar, is the sharpest personal-god claim in this roster — it does not synthesize with the wider Vaishnava cosmos but subordinates it.
figures.provenance: The Vachanamrut (compiled discourses of Sahajanand Swami, early 19th c.); BAPS (Bochasanwasi Akshar Purushottam Swaminarayan Sanstha) institutional theology, founded 1907.

figures.gloss: Mirra Alfassa (1878-1973), Sri Aurobindo's collaborator at the Pondicherry ashram, whom he identified as the Shakti — the Divine Mother's own active, incarnate force — rather than merely a senior disciple. After his death in 1950 she led the ashram alone and founded Auroville (1968) as the experimental city meant to embody the coming 'supramental' transformation.
figures.facet: Her authority derives from Aurobindo's own theological identification of her, not from independent revelation — she is divinized by another founder's doctrine rather than by direct personal claim, an unusual mechanism of female sanctification within this roster.
figures.provenance: Sri Aurobindo, writings identifying the Mother's cosmic function (1920s-40s); The Mother, Words of the Mother; Auroville Foundation charter (1968).

figures.gloss: Sudhamani Idamannel (b. 1953), known as Mata Amritanandamayi or 'Amma', received by devotees as an embodiment of Devi. Her signature rite is darshan by physical embrace — she has personally hugged an estimated tens of millions of people at public programs worldwide through her Mata Amritanandamayi Math.
figures.facet: Where most gurus in this roster transmit through word, technique, or image, her defining religious act is unmediated physical contact — the embrace itself, not a teaching or initiation, is the darshan.
figures.provenance: Mata Amritanandamayi Math institutional literature; biographical accounts of her public embrace programs; independent journalistic coverage.

figures.gloss: Aurobindo Ghose (1872-1950), Cambridge-educated Bengali revolutionary turned yogi, who after a period of political imprisonment (1908) withdrew to Pondicherry and developed Integral Yoga — a doctrine of the coming 'supramental' descent that would evolve human consciousness itself, not merely liberate the individual soul from it.
figures.facet: He reframes liberation as a stage in cosmic evolution still in progress rather than a static goal already fully available — the Supermind is not yet come, making his doctrine uniquely future-oriented against the roster's mostly present-tense realization claims.
figures.provenance: Sri Aurobindo, The Life Divine (1939-40) and The Synthesis of Yoga; Sri Aurobindo Ashram institutional literature, Pondicherry (founded 1926).

figures.gloss: Chandra Mohan Jain (1931-1990), known successively as Acharya Rajneesh, Bhagwan Shree Rajneesh, and Osho, who taught that enlightenment came through embracing rather than renouncing sensuality, wealth, and pleasure, deliberately overturning the ascetic norms of Indian monastic tradition. His Pune ashram and later Oregon commune (Rajneeshpuram, 1981-85) drew thousands of Western followers before controversy and his 1985 deportation from the United States.
figures.facet: He built provocation itself into the religious method — deliberate violation of surrounding taboos (sex, wealth, laughter) was not incidental scandal but the stated technique of awakening, making transgression his equivalent of another teacher's meditation instructions.
figures.provenance: Osho's own discourses, collected in over 600 published volumes; Hugh Urban, Zorba the Buddha: Sex, Spirituality, and Capitalism in the Global Osho Movement (2015).

figures.gloss: Lekhraj Kripalani (1876-1969), a Sindhi diamond merchant who in the 1930s began entering trance states in which he reported the incorporeal Supreme Soul (Shiva Baba) speaking through his body; devotees came to call him Brahma Baba and organized around his channeled discourses ('Murlis') as the founding medium of the Brahma Kumaris.
figures.facet: He is venerated not for what he himself taught as a person but as the vacated vessel through which another being spoke — his own biography is deliberately subordinated to the incorporeal Soul using him, inverting the usual founder-centered model of new religious authority.
figures.provenance: Brahma Kumaris institutional history and the Avyakt Murlis (channeled discourses attributed to Shiva Baba through Brahma Baba, 1930s-1969).

figures.gloss: Nirmala Srivastava (1923-2011), known to devotees as Shri Mataji, who founded Sahaja Yoga in Delhi in 1970 and taught that she could bestow mass, instantaneous 'Self-Realization' — the awakening of the dormant Kundalini through the crown — on entire auditoriums at once, without the years of ascetic preparation traditionally required.
figures.facet: She collapses the traditional guru's individual, years-long transmission into a single public event repeatable at scale — a democratization of an esoteric technique her own movement presents as the sign of the present age's spiritual urgency.
figures.provenance: Shri Mataji Nirmala Devi, Meta Modern Era (1995); Sahaja Yoga institutional literature and public program records.

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: Shiva Baba must forcefully intervene in the material world at the absolute end of the Kalpa cycle (the Confluence Age) to destroy the old tree and plant the seed of the new one.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: ISKCON explicitly subordinates the traditional formless Brahman, redefining it merely as the shining bodily effulgence radiating from Krishna's supreme personal form.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Yogananda explicitly taught that Mahavatar Babaji and Jesus Christ work together in continuous communion to send the science of Kriya Yoga to save the West.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an underlying, unmanifest totality of pure potential and consciousness from which all material reality and localized minds continuously emanate.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Osho's ideal explicitly rejects all spirit-matter dualism and asceticism, demanding the simultaneous indulgence of earthly material desires and absolute spiritual detachment.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both envision the ultimate divine as a singular, invisible point of pure, unblemished spiritual essence existing entirely outside the corrupted material cycle.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: While both use the term kalpa to denote cosmic time, the Brahma Kumaris compress this into a hyper-fatalistic, infinitely repeating cycle of exactly 5,000 years.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an innate, dormant piece of divine potential residing within the human form, requiring specific esoteric triggering to awaken, rise, and grant liberation.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme incarnate figures destined to intervene at the climax of historical degradation, ruthlessly destroying systemic corruption and restoring cosmic moral order.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both function as an impersonal, omnipresent, un-namable fundamental law of nature that spontaneously harmonizes all existence when a practitioner aligns with it effortlessly.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both utilize the serpent archetype as the primordial, animating current of life energy, though one is internalized in the human spine and the other externalized in the landscape.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are envisioned as exclusively supreme, hyper-personal creators who demand absolute devotion and operate totally distinct from the pantheons of other gods.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Osho's concept mirrors the Daoist integration of opposites, positing that a complete human must perfectly balance materialistic celebration and silent meditation.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both function as immortal, mythic originators of esoteric sciences, dwelling outside normal historical time to disseminate ancient transformative techniques to elite initiates.
Intra-tradition: direct, historically documented guru-disciple relationship — the founding transmission of the whole modern-Vedanta branch of this roster.
Intra-tradition: SRF's own lineage narrative has Babaji transmit Kriya Yoga through Lahiri Mahasaya to Yogananda, as recorded in the movement's canonical text.
Intra-tradition: Prabhupada is the founder-acharya and author behind the roster's Krishna-as-Supreme-Godhead theology already cited via his Bhagavad-gita As It Is.
Intra-tradition: direct, historically documented collaboration and spiritual partnership at the Pondicherry ashram.
Intra-tradition: Shiva Baba speaks through Brahma Baba's body by the movement's own founding account.
Intra-tradition: Brahma Baba's mediumship marks the Confluence Age moment in the Kalpa Tree cosmology he transmitted.
Intra-tradition: Osho is the direct author of the Zorba-the-Buddha concept already in this roster, from his own discourses.

Both claim the movement's founder-deity as the singular Supreme Personality standing above all other avatars — a structural rhyme between ISKCON's Krishna theology and the Swaminarayan sampradaya's Purushottam, arrived at independently.
Intra-tradition: she is the founder-transmitter of the mass Kundalini-awakening practice already in this roster, cited from her own book.
Both are women revered by devotees as living embodiments of the Divine Mother/Devi — a rare pattern of female avatar-hood within this modern Hindu-derived landscape.

Both founded enduring Western institutions (ISKCON, 1966; the TM movement, from 1959) built around a single elevated personal doctrine — pillars of the same 1960s-70s guru wave, though housed in separate corpus traditions.

Same deity, inverted theological rank: mainstream Vaishnavism holds Kṛṣṇa as Viṣṇu's avatāra, while ISKCON's Gauḍīya doctrine makes him svayaṃ bhagavān, the original Godhead of whom Viṣṇu is the expansion.

Same serpent-power doctrine, democratized: Śākta tantra's kuṇḍalinī rises only after years of guarded discipline under a guru, while Sahaja Yoga's Self-Realization grants the identical awakening instantly, in public, to mass audiences.

Brahma Kumaris names its incorporeal 'Supreme Soul' after the Puranic Shiva but strips away murti, temple, and myth entirely — a bare identity-claim on the name with none of the anthropomorphic content retained, held at the loosest tier.

dossier.essence: A cluster of modern new religious movements that grew from Hindu, yogic, Vedantic, and tantric roots (from the later nineteenth century onward) and globalized, especially into the West — among them Transcendental Meditation, the Brahma Kumaris, Self-Realization Fellowship, Ananda Marga, Osho/Rajneesh, Sahaja Yoga, and ISKCON (the Hare Krishnas). They typically pair a charismatic living guru or founder-vessel of the divine with a portable spiritual technique — meditation, mantra, kriya, or kundalini awakening — offered across cultures. They are not one doctrine but a family sharing this reformist, exportable shape.
dossier.cosmos: Cosmologies differ sharply. ISKCON teaches a personalist Vaishnava theism in which Krishna is the supreme source and eternal souls transmigrate until liberated by devotion; the Brahma Kumaris hold a soul–matter dualism and a fatalistic 5,000-year world-cycle with God as an incorporeal point of light; Transcendental Meditation reframes pure consciousness as a scientized 'unified field'; Osho's vision is non-dual and anti-ascetic. What recurs is the reinterpretation of classical Hindu concepts (Brahman, kundalini, kalpa, avatar) for a modern, often universalist and 'scientific' audience.
dossier.practice: Each centers on an initiatory technique and guru-devotion: mantra meditation (TM), Raja Yoga with celibacy and vegetarianism (Brahma Kumaris), Kriya Yoga (Self-Realization Fellowship), dynamic meditation and sannyas (Osho), kundalini 'self-realization' (Sahaja Yoga), and deity worship with chanting of the Hare Krishna mantra and temple life (ISKCON). Several have been globally influential and, in several cases, publicly controversial.
dossier.canon: Founder-authored texts rather than a shared scripture — Prabhupada's 'Bhagavad-gita As It Is' (1968), the Brahma Kumaris' channeled 'Murlis,' Maharishi Mahesh Yogi's 'Science of Being and Art of Living' (1963), and Paramahansa Yogananda's 'Autobiography of a Yogi' (1946) — layered over classical sources such as the Bhagavad Gita, Upanishads, and Yoga Sutras.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the portable technique and the living guru — liberation repackaged as a transmissible, marketable method that crosses cultures, detaching yogic practice from its inherited social and ritual context.
sources: Lola Williamson, 'Transcendent in America: Hindu-Inspired Meditation Movements as New Religion' (2010)
Hugh B. Urban, 'Zorba the Buddha: Sex, Spirituality, and the New Global Capitalism in the Rajneesh Movement' (2015)
Elizabeth De Michelis, 'A History of Modern Yoga: Patanjali and Western Esotericism' (2004)
Kim Knott, 'Hinduism: A Very Short Introduction' (2nd ed., 2016)
